title: Efficacy of mRNA Vaccines Against SARS-CoV-2 Variants of Concern: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
abstract: Background: The emergence of SARS-CoV-2 variants of concern (VOCs) has raised questions about the continued efficacy of mRNA vaccines. We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis to evaluate the effectiveness of BNT162b2 and mRNA-1273 vaccines against Alpha, Beta, Delta, and Omicron variants. Methods: We searched PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane databases through December 2024. Studies reporting vaccine effectiveness (VE) against confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection, hospitalization, or death were included. Random-effects meta-analysis was performed. Results: Forty-seven studies encompassing 12.8 million participants were included. Two-dose VE against symptomatic infection was 92% (95% CI: 89-94%) for Alpha, 78% (72-83%) for Delta, and 52% (45-59%) for Omicron. VE against hospitalization remained above 80% for all variants after two doses and above 90% after booster doses. Waning immunity was observed after 6 months, with VE against infection declining by 15-25 percentage points. Booster doses restored VE to near-primary series levels. Conclusions: mRNA vaccines maintain strong protection against severe disease across VOCs, though effectiveness against infection wanes over time and varies by variant. Booster vaccinations are critical for sustaining protection.

title: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors in Advanced Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: Five-Year Survival Outcomes
abstract: Purpose: To report 5-year overall survival (OS) outcomes from the phase III KEYNOTE-024 extension study evaluating pembrolizumab monotherapy versus platinum-based chemotherapy in treatment-naive advanced NSCLC with PD-L1 tumor proportion score ≥50%. Methods: Patients were randomized 1:1 to pembrolizumab 200mg Q3W (n=154) or investigator-choice chemotherapy (n=151). Crossover from chemotherapy to pembrolizumab was permitted after progression. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival; OS was a key secondary endpoint. Results: At a median follow-up of 59.9 months, the 5-year OS rate was 31.9% with pembrolizumab versus 16.3% with chemotherapy (HR 0.62, 95% CI: 0.48-0.81). Median OS was 26.3 months vs 13.4 months. Among pembrolizumab-treated patients who completed 35 cycles (2 years), the 3-year OS rate from completion was 81.4%. Grade 3-5 immune-related adverse events occurred in 31.2% of pembrolizumab-treated patients. Conclusions: Pembrolizumab monotherapy demonstrates durable long-term survival benefit in advanced NSCLC with high PD-L1 expression. These results support first-line pembrolizumab as a standard of care in this population.

title: Gut Microbiome Composition and Response to Immunotherapy in Metastatic Melanoma
abstract: Background: The gut microbiome has been implicated in modulating responses to immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs). We investigated the association between baseline gut microbiome composition and clinical outcomes in patients receiving anti-PD-1 therapy for metastatic melanoma. Methods: Stool samples were collected from 338 patients prior to initiating pembrolizumab or nivolumab. 16S rRNA gene sequencing and shotgun metagenomics were performed. Patients were classified as responders (CR/PR) or non-responders (SD/PD) based on RECIST v1.1 at 12 weeks. Results: Responders (n=142) demonstrated significantly higher alpha diversity (Shannon index 3.8 vs 2.9, P<0.001) and enrichment of Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Bifidobacterium longum, and Akkermansia muciniphila. Non-responders were enriched for Bacteroides fragilis and Fusobacterium nucleatum. Functional analysis revealed enrichment of short-chain fatty acid biosynthesis pathways in responders. Patients in the highest tertile of Faecalibacterium abundance had significantly longer progression-free survival (HR 0.52, P=0.003). Conclusions: Gut microbiome composition is associated with immunotherapy response in melanoma. These findings support ongoing clinical trials of microbiome-based interventions to enhance ICI efficacy.

title: CRISPR-Cas9 Gene Editing for Sickle Cell Disease: Phase I/II Clinical Trial Results
abstract: Background: Sickle cell disease (SCD) is caused by a single point mutation in the HBB gene. We report results from a phase I/II clinical trial of CTX001, an ex vivo CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing therapy targeting the BCL11A enhancer to reactivate fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production. Methods: Patients aged 12-35 years with severe SCD and ≥2 vaso-occlusive crises per year were enrolled. CD34+ hematopoietic stem cells were edited and reinfused after myeloablative conditioning with busulfan. Primary endpoints were safety and engraftment; secondary endpoints included HbF levels and vaso-occlusive events. Results: Among 44 treated patients followed for a median of 24.6 months, all achieved successful engraftment. Total hemoglobin increased from a mean of 8.5 g/dL to 13.7 g/dL, with HbF comprising 40.2% of total hemoglobin. Forty of 44 patients (90.9%) were free of vaso-occlusive crises post-treatment. Grade 3-4 adverse events were primarily related to myeloablative conditioning. No off-target editing events were detected by whole-genome sequencing. Conclusions: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing via CTX001 produces sustained increases in fetal hemoglobin and near-elimination of vaso-occlusive crises in SCD patients.

title: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and Cardiovascular Outcomes in Type 2 Diabetes: Updated Meta-Analysis
abstract: Objective: To evaluate the cardiovascular effects of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs) in patients with type 2 diabetes through an updated systematic review and meta-analysis of cardiovascular outcome trials. Methods: We searched PubMed, Embase, and ClinicalTrials.gov for randomized controlled trials comparing GLP-1 RAs with placebo in type 2 diabetes patients, reporting major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE). Data were pooled using random-effects models. Results: Eight trials comprising 60,080 participants were included. GLP-1 RAs reduced MACE by 14% (HR 0.86, 95% CI: 0.80-0.93, P<0.001), cardiovascular death by 12% (HR 0.88, 0.81-0.96), non-fatal myocardial infarction by 9% (HR 0.91, 0.84-1.00), and non-fatal stroke by 16% (HR 0.84, 0.76-0.93). All-cause mortality was reduced by 12% (HR 0.88, 0.82-0.94). Benefits were consistent across subgroups defined by baseline cardiovascular disease, HbA1c, BMI, and eGFR. GLP-1 RAs also reduced body weight by a mean of 3.2 kg and HbA1c by 0.5%. Conclusions: GLP-1 RAs significantly reduce cardiovascular events and mortality in type 2 diabetes. These benefits extend beyond glycemic control and support the preferential use of GLP-1 RAs in patients with or at risk for cardiovascular disease.

title: Amyloid-Beta Clearance with Lecanemab in Early Alzheimer's Disease: 18-Month Extension Results
abstract: Background: Lecanemab, a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting soluble amyloid-beta protofibrils, demonstrated clinical benefit in the CLARITY AD trial. We report 18-month open-label extension data. Methods: Participants who completed CLARITY AD (n=1,385) entered the open-label extension receiving lecanemab 10 mg/kg biweekly. Amyloid PET, plasma biomarkers, CDR-SB, ADAS-Cog14, and ADCS-MCI-ADL were assessed every 6 months. Results: At month 18 of the extension, 78% of participants achieved amyloid-negative status (Centiloid <30). Mean amyloid PET Centiloid decreased from 37.2 at extension baseline to 18.5. Plasma p-tau217 declined by 52% from extension baseline. The rate of CDR-SB worsening during the extension was 0.72 points/year in participants who received continuous lecanemab versus 1.04 points/year in those who switched from placebo (P=0.008). ARIA-E occurred in 12.3% and ARIA-H microhemorrhage in 14.1% during the extension. Conclusions: Continued lecanemab treatment produces sustained amyloid clearance and slows cognitive decline. Earlier initiation of treatment appears to confer greater long-term benefit.

title: Antibiotic Resistance Patterns in Urinary Tract Infections: A Global Surveillance Study 2020-2024
abstract: Objectives: To characterize global antibiotic resistance patterns in uropathogens causing community-acquired urinary tract infections (CA-UTIs) from 2020 to 2024. Methods: We analyzed 245,000 urine culture isolates from 180 clinical laboratories across 42 countries through the SMART surveillance network. Minimum inhibitory concentrations were determined by broth microdilution. Results: Escherichia coli (68.2%), Klebsiella pneumoniae (12.4%), and Proteus mirabilis (5.8%) were the most common uropathogens. E. coli resistance rates were: ampicillin 62.4%, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 34.8%, ciprofloxacin 28.1%, ceftriaxone 18.3%, nitrofurantoin 2.8%, and fosfomycin 1.4%. Extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL) production was detected in 22.1% of E. coli and 31.6% of K. pneumoniae isolates. Carbapenem resistance was found in 1.2% of E. coli and 8.4% of K. pneumoniae. Significant regional variation was observed, with highest resistance rates in South Asia and lowest in Northern Europe. Resistance rates increased by 2-5 percentage points compared to the 2015-2019 period. Conclusions: Antibiotic resistance in uropathogens continues to rise globally. Nitrofurantoin and fosfomycin remain reliable empiric options for uncomplicated CA-UTIs. Local antibiograms should guide empiric therapy selection.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Diabetic Retinopathy Screening: Prospective Validation in Primary Care
abstract: Purpose: To prospectively validate an AI system for diabetic retinopathy (DR) screening in primary care settings and compare its performance with ophthalmologist grading. Methods: Adults with diabetes aged ≥18 years presenting to 28 primary care clinics underwent fundus photography. Images were analyzed by the AI system (DeepDR v3.2) and independently graded by two certified ophthalmologists. Adjudication was performed by a retinal specialist for discordant cases. The primary outcome was sensitivity and specificity for detecting referable DR (moderate NPDR or worse). Results: Among 15,842 participants (31,684 eyes), referable DR was present in 2,108 (13.3%). The AI system achieved sensitivity of 95.7% (95% CI: 94.6-96.6%) and specificity of 93.4% (92.9-93.8%) for referable DR. For vision-threatening DR (severe NPDR or PDR), sensitivity was 98.1% and specificity 96.2%. Agreement between AI and ophthalmologist reference was high (kappa 0.87). The AI system identified 23 cases of referable DR missed by initial ophthalmologist grading. Median analysis time per patient was 28 seconds for AI versus 4.2 minutes for ophthalmologist grading. Conclusions: The AI system demonstrates high diagnostic accuracy for DR screening in primary care and may improve access to screening while maintaining quality.

title: Ketamine versus Electroconvulsive Therapy for Treatment-Resistant Depression: Randomized Non-Inferiority Trial
abstract: Background: Both intravenous ketamine and electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) have demonstrated efficacy in treatment-resistant depression (TRD), but they have not been directly compared in a randomized trial. Methods: We conducted a randomized, non-inferiority trial at 12 academic medical centers. Adults with TRD (failure of ≥2 adequate antidepressant trials) were randomized to IV ketamine 0.5 mg/kg twice weekly for 3 weeks (n=195) or ECT three times weekly (n=170). The primary outcome was change in MADRS score at 3 weeks, with a non-inferiority margin of 3 points. Results: Mean MADRS scores decreased by 17.4 points (SD 10.2) with ketamine versus 20.1 points (SD 11.8) with ECT. The between-group difference was 2.7 points (95% CI: 0.3-5.1), meeting the non-inferiority criterion. Response rates (≥50% MADRS reduction) were 52.3% for ketamine versus 63.5% for ECT (P=0.03). Cognitive side effects at 3 weeks were more common with ECT (38.2%) than ketamine (12.8%, P<0.001). Dissociative symptoms with ketamine were generally transient, resolving within 2 hours of infusion. Conclusions: Ketamine was non-inferior to ECT for TRD at 3 weeks. Ketamine may be preferred when cognitive side effects are a concern, though ECT showed numerically higher response rates.

title: CAR-T Cell Therapy for Relapsed/Refractory Multiple Myeloma: Phase II CARTITUDE-4 Extended Follow-Up
abstract: Background: Ciltacabtagene autoleucel (cilta-cel), a BCMA-directed CAR-T cell therapy, showed unprecedented response rates in heavily pretreated multiple myeloma. We report the 3-year follow-up of CARTITUDE-4. Methods: Patients with relapsed or refractory myeloma after 1-3 prior lines of therapy, including a proteasome inhibitor and an immunomodulatory drug, received cilta-cel after lymphodepleting chemotherapy. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival (PFS) versus physician's choice of pomalidomide-bortezomib-dexamethasone or daratumumab-pomalidomide-dexamethasone. Results: Among 208 cilta-cel treated patients, the overall response rate was 84.6%, with 73.1% achieving complete response or better. Minimal residual disease negativity (10^-5) was achieved by 60.6%. At 36 months, PFS was 76.0% versus 48.6% in the standard therapy arm (HR 0.26, 95% CI: 0.18-0.38). Median PFS was not reached with cilta-cel versus 11.8 months with standard therapy. Cytokine release syndrome occurred in 76.1% (grade ≥3: 3.8%), and immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity in 4.8%. Conclusions: Cilta-cel demonstrates durable, deep responses in earlier-line relapsed/refractory myeloma, supporting its use as a transformative treatment option.

title: Maternal SSRI Use During Pregnancy and Neonatal Outcomes: Population-Based Cohort Study
abstract: Objective: To evaluate the association between maternal selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) use during pregnancy and neonatal outcomes, accounting for confounding by indication. Methods: We conducted a population-based cohort study using linked health records from 2015-2023 (n=1,245,678 live births). Exposed pregnancies were defined by SSRI dispensing during gestation. We used propensity score matching with sibling analysis to address confounding. Primary outcomes were preterm birth, small for gestational age (SGA), neonatal adaptation syndrome, and persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN). Results: Among 68,432 SSRI-exposed pregnancies (5.5%), the matched analysis showed modestly increased risk of preterm birth (aOR 1.22, 95% CI: 1.15-1.30), neonatal adaptation syndrome (aOR 2.14, 1.89-2.42), and PPHN (aOR 1.51, 1.12-2.04). No significant association was found with SGA (aOR 1.04, 0.97-1.12). Sibling analyses comparing exposed to unexposed siblings of the same mother yielded similar but attenuated estimates. Sertraline and citalopram had similar risk profiles. Third-trimester exposure carried higher risk of neonatal adaptation syndrome than first-trimester only exposure. Conclusions: SSRI use during pregnancy is associated with modest increases in certain neonatal adverse outcomes. These risks should be weighed against the well-documented harms of untreated maternal depression.

title: Continuous Glucose Monitoring in Type 1 Diabetes: Impact on Glycemic Control and Hypoglycemia
abstract: Aims: To assess the real-world impact of continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) on glycemic outcomes in adults with type 1 diabetes (T1D) transitioning from self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG). Methods: Prospective multicenter observational study of 2,400 adults with T1D initiating CGM across 45 diabetes centers. HbA1c, time in range (TIR, 70-180 mg/dL), time below range (TBR, <70 mg/dL), glycemic variability (CV), and patient-reported outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3, 6, and 12 months. Results: Mean HbA1c decreased from 8.2% to 7.4% at 12 months (P<0.001). TIR increased from 44.2% to 61.8% (P<0.001), exceeding the consensus target of 70% in 38.4% of participants. TBR decreased from 5.8% to 2.9% (P<0.001). Severe hypoglycemic events decreased by 72% (from 0.18 to 0.05 events per patient-year). Glycemic variability (CV) decreased from 42.1% to 35.3%. Diabetes distress scores improved significantly (P<0.001). Benefits were observed across all age groups and baseline HbA1c levels. Conclusions: CGM initiation in T1D leads to clinically significant improvements in glycemic control, marked reduction in hypoglycemia, and improved patient well-being.

title: Psilocybin-Assisted Therapy for Major Depressive Disorder: Phase IIb Dose-Ranging Study
abstract: Background: Psilocybin, a serotonin 2A receptor agonist, has shown promise in treatment-resistant depression. We conducted a phase IIb dose-ranging study in major depressive disorder (MDD). Methods: Adults with moderate-to-severe MDD (MADRS ≥20) were randomized to a single dose of psilocybin 1 mg (control), 10 mg, or 25 mg administered with psychological support. The primary endpoint was change in MADRS score at week 3. All participants received preparation and integration therapy sessions. Results: At week 3, mean MADRS reductions were 8.0 points (1 mg, n=79), 14.3 points (10 mg, n=75), and 17.8 points (25 mg, n=78) (25 mg vs 1 mg: P<0.001; 10 mg vs 1 mg: P=0.002). Response rates were 18.9%, 37.3%, and 48.7%, respectively. Remission rates were 8.9%, 22.7%, and 32.1%. Benefits were sustained through week 12 in the 25 mg group. Adverse events included headache (30.1%), nausea (22.4%), and transient anxiety during the session (15.8%). No serious adverse events related to psilocybin were reported. Suicidal ideation decreased across all active-dose groups. Conclusions: Psilocybin 25 mg with psychological support demonstrated significant and sustained antidepressant effects in MDD, supporting advancement to phase III trials.

title: Fecal Microbiota Transplantation for Recurrent Clostridioides difficile Infection: Long-Term Outcomes
abstract: Background: Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) is effective for recurrent Clostridioides difficile infection (rCDI), but long-term efficacy and safety data are limited. Methods: We conducted a prospective registry study following 812 patients who received FMT for rCDI (≥3 episodes) across 24 centers. FMT was delivered via colonoscopy (62%), capsules (28%), or enema (10%). Primary outcome was CDI recurrence through 5 years. Safety events were systematically collected. Results: The overall cure rate after single FMT was 82.4% at 8 weeks and 77.1% at 1 year. After retreatment, the cumulative cure rate was 92.8% at 1 year. At 5 years, 89.4% of patients remained CDI-free. Independent predictors of failure included immunosuppression (OR 2.4, P<0.001), severe CDI (OR 1.9, P=0.008), and ongoing non-CDI antibiotic use (OR 3.1, P<0.001). Capsule and colonoscopic delivery had similar efficacy. Serious adverse events were rare (2.1%) and included one aspiration event and two cases of bacteremia. No cases of transmitted infections were identified with enhanced donor screening. Conclusions: FMT provides durable cure for rCDI with an acceptable safety profile. Most failures occur within the first year and respond to repeat FMT.

title: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Low-Risk Patients: Five-Year Follow-Up of PARTNER 3
abstract: Background: Transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) was non-inferior to surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) in low-surgical-risk patients at 2 years. We report 5-year outcomes. Methods: In this randomized trial, patients with severe aortic stenosis and low surgical risk (STS-PROM <4%) received TAVR with a balloon-expandable valve (n=503) or SAVR (n=497). The primary endpoint was the composite of death from any cause, stroke, or rehospitalization at 5 years. Results: At 5 years, the primary composite endpoint occurred in 22.8% of TAVR patients versus 27.2% of SAVR patients (HR 0.81, 95% CI: 0.63-1.04, P=0.10). All-cause mortality was 10.2% vs 12.4% (P=0.31). Disabling stroke rates were 2.4% vs 3.6% (P=0.30). The rate of new permanent pacemaker implantation was 7.4% with TAVR versus 4.8% with SAVR. Moderate or severe paravalvular regurgitation was present in 3.5% of TAVR patients at 5 years versus 0.6% of SAVR patients. Structural valve deterioration occurred in 2.8% vs 1.4%. Valve hemodynamics remained stable in both groups. Conclusions: TAVR and SAVR show comparable 5-year outcomes in low-risk patients. Longer follow-up is needed to assess valve durability beyond 5 years, particularly regarding structural valve deterioration.

title: Sleep Duration, Quality, and Risk of Cardiovascular Disease: UK Biobank Prospective Analysis
abstract: Objectives: To examine the associations between sleep duration, sleep quality, and incident cardiovascular disease (CVD), accounting for genetic predisposition. Methods: We analyzed 402,585 UK Biobank participants (aged 40-69) free of CVD at baseline. Self-reported sleep duration, insomnia frequency, chronotype, daytime sleepiness, and snoring were assessed. A healthy sleep score (0-5) was constructed. Incident CVD (MI, stroke, heart failure) was ascertained through hospital records and death registries over a median 12.4-year follow-up. Polygenic risk scores for CVD were calculated. Results: Compared with 7-8 hours, short sleep (<6 hours) was associated with increased CVD risk (HR 1.20, 95% CI: 1.14-1.27), as was long sleep (>9 hours, HR 1.33, 1.24-1.43). A healthy sleep score of 5 versus 0-1 was associated with 42% lower CVD risk (HR 0.58, 0.52-0.65), independent of genetic risk. The population-attributable fraction for unhealthy sleep patterns was estimated at 8.4% of CVD cases. Favorable sleep patterns partially offset high genetic risk: participants with high genetic risk but healthy sleep had similar CVD rates to those with intermediate genetic risk and poor sleep. Conclusions: Healthy sleep patterns are associated with substantially lower CVD risk, even among those with high genetic predisposition. Sleep optimization represents a modifiable target for cardiovascular prevention.

title: Ozempic (Semaglutide) for Obesity-Related Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction: SELECT-HFpEF Results
abstract: Background: Obesity is highly prevalent in heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) and contributes to symptoms and functional limitation. We evaluated semaglutide in patients with obesity-related HFpEF. Methods: In this double-blind trial, 529 patients with HFpEF (LVEF ≥45%), BMI ≥30, and NYHA class II-IV were randomized to weekly subcutaneous semaglutide 2.4 mg or placebo for 52 weeks. The dual primary endpoints were change in KCCQ-CSS (symptoms) and body weight. Key secondary endpoints included 6-minute walk distance, CRP, and NT-proBNP. Results: Semaglutide improved KCCQ-CSS by 7.8 points versus placebo (95% CI: 4.8-10.9, P<0.001) and reduced body weight by 10.7% versus 1.4% (difference -9.4%, P<0.001). Six-minute walk distance increased by 21.5 meters (P<0.001). CRP decreased by 38.2% and NT-proBNP by 15.6% with semaglutide. Hierarchical composite of cardiovascular death, heart failure events, and KCCQ-CSS change favored semaglutide (win ratio 1.72, P<0.001). Serious adverse events were similar between groups (16.6% vs 17.7%). Gastrointestinal events were more common with semaglutide. Conclusions: Semaglutide significantly improved symptoms, physical function, and exercise capacity in patients with obesity-related HFpEF, with concurrent large reductions in body weight and inflammation.

title: Deep Learning for Early Detection of Pancreatic Cancer from CT Imaging: Multi-Institutional Validation
abstract: Purpose: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) has poor prognosis partly due to late detection. We developed and validated a deep learning model for early PDAC detection on routine abdominal CT scans. Methods: A 3D convolutional neural network was trained on 3,208 CT scans (1,604 PDAC, 1,604 normal) from three institutions. External validation was performed on 1,045 scans from two held-out institutions. We also tested the model on 800 pre-diagnostic CTs from patients who developed PDAC 6-18 months after imaging. Results: On external validation, the model achieved AUROC 0.98 for PDAC detection, with sensitivity 94.3% and specificity 95.1%. For pre-diagnostic CTs (scans obtained before clinical diagnosis), the model detected 35.4% of cancers at 12-18 months before diagnosis and 71.2% at 6-12 months before diagnosis. The model identified subtle pancreatic duct dilation, parenchymal changes, and early mass effect invisible to initial clinical reads. False positive rate was 4.9% on normal scans. Performance was consistent across CT manufacturers and imaging protocols. Conclusions: Deep learning can detect pancreatic cancer on CT imaging up to 18 months before clinical diagnosis, potentially enabling earlier intervention and improved survival.

title: Sodium-Glucose Co-Transporter 2 Inhibitors in Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction: Individual Patient Data Meta-Analysis
abstract: Background: SGLT2 inhibitors have shown benefits in heart failure trials, but effects in specific subgroups remain uncertain. We performed an individual patient data (IPD) meta-analysis of major heart failure trials. Methods: IPD from DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced, DELIVER, and SOLOIST-WHF were harmonized (n=21,947). The primary outcome was cardiovascular death or first heart failure hospitalization. Prespecified subgroup analyses included diabetes status, age, sex, race, eGFR, LVEF, and NYHA class. Results: SGLT2 inhibitors reduced the primary outcome by 23% overall (HR 0.77, 95% CI: 0.72-0.82, P<0.001). Benefits were consistent regardless of diabetes status (with diabetes: HR 0.77; without: HR 0.78, P-interaction=0.84), sex (men: HR 0.76; women: HR 0.79, P-interaction=0.64), and across the LVEF spectrum (LVEF ≤30%: HR 0.75; 31-40%: HR 0.78; >40%: HR 0.79, P-interaction=0.71). The number needed to treat to prevent one primary event over 18 months was 21. Benefits appeared within the first 28 days of treatment. SGLT2 inhibitors reduced all-cause mortality by 13% (HR 0.87, P=0.003). Conclusions: SGLT2 inhibitors provide consistent cardiovascular benefits across the full spectrum of heart failure, independent of diabetes status and ejection fraction.

title: Long COVID Neurological Manifestations: A Prospective Cohort Study with Neuroimaging
abstract: Background: Neurological symptoms are common in long COVID, but their neurobiological correlates remain poorly understood. We prospectively characterized neurological manifestations of long COVID using multimodal neuroimaging and neuropsychological testing. Methods: Patients with confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection and persistent neurological symptoms ≥12 weeks post-infection (n=225) and matched recovered controls (n=150) underwent brain MRI with volumetric analysis, diffusion tensor imaging (DTI), resting-state fMRI, FDG-PET, neuropsychological testing, and blood biomarkers. Results: Compared with controls, long COVID patients showed reduced gray matter volume in orbitofrontal cortex (-1.8%, P<0.001), hippocampus (-1.2%, P=0.003), and insula (-1.5%, P=0.001). DTI revealed reduced fractional anisotropy in multiple white matter tracts. FDG-PET demonstrated hypometabolism in brainstem and medial temporal regions. Cognitive impairment (≥1.5 SD below norms) was present in 42.7% of patients, predominantly affecting attention, processing speed, and executive function. Serum neurofilament light chain was elevated in 28.4% and correlated with white matter changes (r=0.41, P<0.001). At 12-month follow-up, 58.2% showed improvement in cognitive scores, but structural brain changes persisted. Conclusions: Long COVID is associated with measurable structural and functional brain changes that correlate with cognitive symptoms. Persistent neuroimaging abnormalities despite symptomatic improvement raise concerns about long-term neurological sequelae.

title: Bispecific Antibodies in Relapsed/Refractory Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma: Real-World Outcomes
abstract: Background: Bispecific antibodies targeting CD20 and CD3 have shown efficacy in relapsed/refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (R/R DLBCL) in clinical trials. We report real-world outcomes with glofitamab and epcoritamab. Methods: Retrospective multicenter analysis of 342 patients with R/R DLBCL treated with glofitamab (n=186) or epcoritamab (n=156) across 38 centers. Patients had received median 3 prior lines (range 2-7); 44.2% had prior CAR-T therapy. Outcomes were assessed per Lugano criteria. Results: Overall response rates were 52.7% for glofitamab and 48.1% for epcoritamab. Complete response rates were 36.0% and 31.4%, respectively. Median PFS was 6.2 months (glofitamab) and 5.4 months (epcoritamab). Among complete responders, 12-month duration of response was 64.3%. Cytokine release syndrome occurred in 47.8% (grade ≥3: 4.1%). Post-CAR-T patients had lower response rates (38.5%) compared with CAR-T naive patients (57.2%, P=0.003). Patients with non-germinal center B-cell subtype had inferior outcomes. Tocilizumab use for CRS management did not impact anti-tumor efficacy. Conclusions: Bispecific antibodies show meaningful real-world activity in R/R DLBCL, including in post-CAR-T patients, though outcomes are somewhat lower than clinical trial results.

title: Statin Therapy and Dementia Risk: Individual Participant Data Meta-Analysis of Randomized Trials
abstract: Background: Observational studies suggest statins may reduce dementia risk, but randomized trial evidence is inconclusive. We performed an individual participant data (IPD) meta-analysis to assess whether statin therapy reduces incident dementia. Methods: IPD from 21 randomized statin trials (n=143,890 participants, median follow-up 4.9 years) were obtained through the Cholesterol Treatment Trialists Collaboration. Incident dementia was identified through serious adverse event reports, hospital records, and death certificates. Analyses were stratified by baseline LDL cholesterol, age, and statin type. Results: Dementia occurred in 2,802 statin-allocated versus 2,944 control-allocated participants (RR 0.95, 95% CI: 0.90-1.00, P=0.057). Among participants aged ≥70 years (n=41,204), statin use was associated with a modest reduction (RR 0.92, 95% CI: 0.85-0.99, P=0.026). No significant effect was found in younger participants. More intensive LDL lowering was not associated with greater dementia risk reduction. There was no evidence that statins caused cognitive impairment (standardized cognitive test scores: mean difference 0.01, P=0.78). Results were consistent across statin types and baseline cognitive status. Conclusions: Statin therapy does not adversely affect cognition and may modestly reduce dementia risk, particularly in older adults. These findings do not support withholding statins due to cognitive concerns.

title: Wearable ECG Monitoring for Atrial Fibrillation Detection: SCREEN-AF Randomized Trial
abstract: Background: Screening for atrial fibrillation (AF) using wearable devices could enable earlier anticoagulation and stroke prevention. We conducted a randomized trial of continuous wearable ECG monitoring for AF detection. Methods: Adults ≥65 years without known AF but with CHA2DS2-VASc score ≥2 were randomized to continuous wearable patch ECG monitoring for 14 days repeated quarterly (n=2,650) or usual care (n=2,638). The primary endpoint was new AF detection at 12 months. Key secondary endpoints included anticoagulant initiation, stroke, and major bleeding. Results: New AF was detected in 6.8% of the monitoring group versus 2.1% in usual care (difference 4.7%, 95% CI: 3.7-5.7%, P<0.001). The monitoring group had a 3.2-fold higher rate of anticoagulant initiation (8.4% vs 2.6%, P<0.001). Over a median 2.5-year follow-up, stroke or systemic embolism occurred in 1.4% of the monitoring group versus 2.3% of usual care (HR 0.61, 95% CI: 0.42-0.88, P=0.008). Major bleeding rates were 1.2% vs 0.8% (P=0.16). Incidental findings requiring follow-up occurred in 3.4% of monitored patients. Conclusions: Systematic wearable ECG monitoring in older adults at risk for AF significantly improves detection rates and reduces stroke risk, supporting screening implementation in this population.

title: mRNA Vaccine Platform for Personalized Cancer Neoantigen Immunotherapy: Phase I Results
abstract: Background: Personalized mRNA vaccines targeting tumor-specific neoantigens represent a novel immunotherapy approach. We report phase I results of an individualized mRNA neoantigen vaccine (mRNA-4157/V940) combined with pembrolizumab in patients with resected high-risk melanoma. Methods: Patients with completely resected stage III/IV melanoma received up to 9 doses of individualized mRNA vaccine encoding up to 34 patient-specific neoantigens plus pembrolizumab 200 mg Q3W for up to 1 year. Primary endpoints were safety and neoantigen-specific T cell responses. Key secondary endpoint was recurrence-free survival (RFS). Results: Among 107 treated patients, the vaccine was well-tolerated. Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 14.8% (most were immune-related from pembrolizumab). Neoantigen-specific T cell responses were detected in 79.4% of evaluable patients, with de novo responses to 35% of targeted neoantigens. At 2 years, RFS was 78.6% with the combination versus 62.2% with pembrolizumab alone (from a concurrent randomized cohort, HR 0.56, 95% CI: 0.31-1.08). Patients with detectable neoantigen-specific T cell responses had superior RFS compared to non-responders (HR 0.32, P=0.01). Conclusions: Individualized mRNA neoantigen vaccines are feasible, immunogenic, and show promising signals of clinical benefit in combination with checkpoint inhibitors for melanoma.

title: Exercise Prescription in Chronic Kidney Disease: Effect on eGFR Decline and Cardiovascular Risk
abstract: Objective: To evaluate the effect of structured exercise on kidney function decline and cardiovascular risk factors in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) stages 3-4. Methods: Multicenter randomized controlled trial of 520 patients with CKD (eGFR 15-59 mL/min/1.73m²) assigned to supervised exercise (150 min/week moderate-intensity aerobic + resistance training, n=261) or usual care (n=259) for 12 months. The primary outcome was annualized eGFR change. Secondary outcomes included proteinuria, blood pressure, arterial stiffness, cardiorespiratory fitness (VO2peak), and quality of life. Results: The exercise group showed slower eGFR decline (-1.1 mL/min/1.73m²/year vs -2.4 mL/min/1.73m²/year, difference 1.3, 95% CI: 0.6-2.0, P<0.001). Proteinuria decreased by 18.4% in the exercise group (P=0.02). Systolic blood pressure was lower by 4.8 mmHg (P<0.001), and pulse wave velocity decreased by 0.6 m/s (P=0.003). VO2peak improved by 2.8 mL/kg/min (P<0.001). Quality of life scores improved significantly in physical functioning and vitality domains. No increase in adverse events was observed in the exercise group. Adherence to the exercise program was 72.4%. Conclusions: Structured exercise significantly slows eGFR decline and improves cardiovascular risk factors in CKD stages 3-4, supporting exercise as a therapeutic intervention in CKD management.

title: Liquid Biopsy for Minimal Residual Disease Detection in Early-Stage Breast Cancer: ctDNA Analysis
abstract: Background: Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) detection after curative-intent therapy may identify minimal residual disease (MRD) in breast cancer. We evaluated a tumor-informed ctDNA assay for MRD detection and recurrence prediction. Methods: Prospective cohort of 1,127 patients with stage I-III breast cancer. Tumor whole-exome sequencing identified patient-specific mutations, and personalized ctDNA panels tracked 16-50 variants in serial plasma samples collected post-surgery and every 3 months for 3 years. Primary endpoint was the association between ctDNA positivity and distant recurrence-free survival (DRFS). Results: ctDNA was detected post-surgery in 8.4% of patients. ctDNA-positive patients had markedly worse DRFS (HR 17.8, 95% CI: 11.2-28.3, P<0.001). Lead time from ctDNA detection to clinical recurrence was a median of 8.9 months (range 2.1-24.6). Sensitivity for predicting eventual recurrence was 88.4%, specificity 97.6%. Among ctDNA-positive patients, molecular subtype influenced time to recurrence: HR+ tumors showed longer lead time (11.2 months) versus HER2+ (7.4 months) and triple-negative (5.8 months). ctDNA clearance during adjuvant therapy was associated with improved outcomes (HR 0.21, P<0.001). Conclusions: Tumor-informed ctDNA analysis detects MRD with high accuracy in early breast cancer, with substantial lead time before clinical recurrence, potentially enabling timely intervention.

title: Mediterranean Diet Supplemented with Extra Virgin Olive Oil and Cognitive Decline in Older Adults
abstract: Objective: To evaluate whether a Mediterranean diet supplemented with extra virgin olive oil (EVOO) reduces cognitive decline in older adults at high cardiovascular risk. Methods: Secondary analysis of the PREDIMED-Plus trial randomizing 4,152 participants aged 55-75 with overweight/obesity and metabolic syndrome to an energy-restricted Mediterranean diet with EVOO (≥50 mL/day) or a control low-fat diet. Neurocognitive assessments (MMSE, Clock Drawing, Trail Making, Verbal Fluency) were performed at baseline and annually for 5 years. Results: At 5 years, the Mediterranean diet group showed significantly less decline in MMSE (difference 0.62 points, 95% CI: 0.28-0.96, P<0.001) and Trail Making Test B (difference 8.4 seconds, P=0.003) compared with control. Verbal fluency declined less in the intervention group (P=0.01). MRI substudy (n=320) showed greater preservation of hippocampal volume in the Mediterranean diet group (-2.1% vs -4.4%, P=0.008). Incident MCI was lower in the intervention (12.8% vs 18.4%, HR 0.67, P=0.002). Benefits were more pronounced in APOE ε4 carriers. Plasma oleocanthal metabolites correlated with cognitive preservation (r=0.28, P<0.001). Conclusions: Mediterranean diet enriched with EVOO significantly attenuates cognitive decline and reduces MCI incidence in older adults at cardiovascular risk, with structural brain preservation supporting the clinical findings.

title: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in HER2-Low Metastatic Breast Cancer: Overall Survival Results from DESTINY-Breast04
abstract: Background: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) showed unprecedented progression-free survival benefit in HER2-low metastatic breast cancer in the DESTINY-Breast04 primary analysis. We report final overall survival (OS) results. Methods: Patients with HER2-low (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH-) metastatic breast cancer previously treated with 1-2 lines of chemotherapy were randomized 2:1 to T-DXd 5.4 mg/kg Q3W (n=373) or physician's choice chemotherapy (n=184). The key secondary endpoint was OS. Results: At a median follow-up of 32.0 months, median OS was 23.9 months with T-DXd versus 17.5 months with chemotherapy (HR 0.69, 95% CI: 0.55-0.87, P=0.002). In the HR-positive cohort, median OS was 25.4 vs 18.2 months (HR 0.69). In the HR-negative cohort, median OS was 18.2 vs 8.3 months (HR 0.48, P=0.007). The 2-year OS rate was 47.6% vs 35.5%. Treatment-related interstitial lung disease/pneumonitis occurred in 12.1% (grade ≥3: 1.6%, fatal: 0.5%). Quality of life was maintained or improved with T-DXd. No new safety signals emerged with longer follow-up. Conclusions: T-DXd provides a statistically significant and clinically meaningful overall survival benefit in HER2-low metastatic breast cancer, establishing a new standard of care for this patient population.

title: Physical Activity and All-Cause Mortality: Dose-Response Analysis Using Accelerometry Data
abstract: Objective: To determine the dose-response relationship between objectively measured physical activity and mortality using wrist-worn accelerometers. Methods: Pooled analysis of 96,476 adults aged 40-79 from four prospective cohorts (UK Biobank, NHANES, Norwegian Nord-Trøndelag Health Study, Whitehall II) with wrist accelerometry data. Total activity, moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA), and light physical activity (LPA) were quantified. Mortality was ascertained through national death registries (median follow-up 6.9 years, 3,148 deaths). Results: Higher total activity was associated with lower mortality in a non-linear dose-response fashion. Compared with the least active quintile, the most active quintile had 60% lower mortality (HR 0.40, 95% CI: 0.35-0.46). The steepest reduction occurred between the first and third quintiles. As little as 11 minutes/day of MVPA was associated with 23% lower mortality risk. The benefit plateaued at approximately 35-40 minutes/day of MVPA. Light physical activity also independently reduced mortality: replacing 30 minutes of sedentary time with LPA was associated with 11% lower risk (HR 0.89, 0.85-0.93). Associations were consistent across age, sex, BMI, and comorbidity subgroups. Conclusions: Small amounts of physical activity confer substantial mortality benefits, with the greatest gains from moving out of the least active category. Any activity is better than none, and recommendations should emphasize achievable increases.

title: Sacubitril/Valsartan in Acute Decompensated Heart Failure: PIONEER-HF Extended Follow-Up
abstract: Background: The PIONEER-HF trial showed that in-hospital initiation of sacubitril/valsartan reduced NT-proBNP compared with enalapril in acute decompensated heart failure (ADHF). We present 12-month clinical outcomes. Methods: Patients hospitalized for ADHF with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF ≤40%) who were hemodynamically stabilized were randomized to sacubitril/valsartan 97/103 mg BID (n=440) or enalapril 10 mg BID (n=441). The extended follow-up assessed the composite of cardiovascular death, heart failure rehospitalization, and LVAD implantation or heart transplant listing at 12 months. Results: At 12 months, the composite endpoint occurred in 22.5% of the sacubitril/valsartan group versus 31.2% of the enalapril group (HR 0.68, 95% CI: 0.53-0.88, P=0.003). Heart failure rehospitalization occurred in 17.3% versus 24.8% (HR 0.66, P=0.005). LVEF improved by a mean of 8.4% versus 5.7% (P=0.008). NT-proBNP reductions at 12 months were 52% versus 38% (P<0.001). The benefit of in-hospital initiation was greatest in patients started within 48 hours of stabilization. Hypotension requiring intervention occurred in 12.1% versus 7.4%. Conclusions: In-hospital initiation of sacubitril/valsartan in ADHF improves 12-month clinical outcomes compared with enalapril, supporting early initiation before discharge.

title: Whole Genome Sequencing for Rapid Neonatal Intensive Care Diagnosis: Clinical Utility Study
abstract: Background: Critically ill neonates frequently have undiagnosed genetic conditions that influence management. We evaluated rapid whole genome sequencing (rWGS) as a first-tier test in the NICU. Methods: Prospective multicenter study of 500 consecutively enrolled NICU patients with suspected genetic conditions. Trio rWGS (proband + parents) was performed with a target turnaround time of 5 days. Diagnostic yield, time to diagnosis, and clinical impact were assessed. Conventional genetic testing was performed in parallel. Results: Diagnostic yield of rWGS was 43.4% (217/500), compared with 10.2% for conventional testing ordered in parallel (P<0.001). Median time to diagnosis was 4.8 days with rWGS versus 42 days with conventional testing. Diagnoses spanned 187 distinct genetic conditions, with 34.6% being de novo variants. rWGS changed clinical management in 72.8% of diagnosed patients, including initiation of targeted therapies (23.5%), avoidance of unnecessary procedures (18.4%), and redirection of care goals (16.6%). rWGS identified 12 patients with actionable pharmacogenomic variants. Cost analysis showed savings of $14,265 per diagnosed patient due to reduced downstream testing and hospital stay. Conclusions: Rapid trio WGS has high diagnostic yield and clinical utility as a first-tier test in the NICU, enabling faster diagnoses and more targeted management.

title: Intermittent Fasting Versus Continuous Caloric Restriction for Weight Loss and Metabolic Health: 12-Month RCT
abstract: Objective: To compare the effects of time-restricted eating (TRE) with continuous caloric restriction (CCR) on weight loss and metabolic parameters over 12 months. Methods: Adults with BMI 28-40 kg/m² were randomized to TRE (eating within an 8-hour window, no calorie counting, n=139), CCR (25% caloric deficit, n=137), or control (standard dietary advice, n=71) for 12 months. Primary outcome was change in body weight. Secondary outcomes included body composition (DEXA), insulin sensitivity (HOMA-IR), lipid profile, blood pressure, and inflammatory markers. Results: At 12 months, weight change was -7.2 kg (TRE), -8.1 kg (CCR), and -1.8 kg (control). The difference between TRE and CCR was not significant (0.9 kg, 95% CI: -0.8 to 2.6, P=0.29). Both intervention groups had similar improvements in fat mass (-5.8 vs -6.2 kg), HOMA-IR (-28% vs -31%), triglycerides (-18% vs -22%), and systolic blood pressure (-5.2 vs -5.8 mmHg). Lean mass loss was modestly greater with TRE (-1.4 vs -0.9 kg, P=0.04). Adherence at 12 months was higher for TRE (78.4%) versus CCR (62.8%, P=0.003). Fasting insulin and CRP improved comparably. Conclusions: TRE and CCR produce similar weight loss and metabolic improvements over 12 months, but TRE has better adherence. The modest additional lean mass loss with TRE warrants consideration.

title: Dupilumab in Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease with Type 2 Inflammation: BOREAS Trial Results
abstract: Background: A subgroup of COPD patients exhibit type 2 inflammation with elevated blood eosinophils. We evaluated dupilumab, a monoclonal antibody blocking IL-4/IL-13 signaling, in this population. Methods: In this phase III trial, patients with COPD, blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/μL, and ≥2 moderate/severe exacerbations in the prior year were randomized to dupilumab 300 mg Q2W (n=468) or placebo (n=471) for 52 weeks. All patients received maximal inhaler therapy (ICS/LABA/LAMA). Primary endpoint was annualized rate of moderate-to-severe exacerbations. Results: Dupilumab reduced moderate-to-severe exacerbations by 30% versus placebo (rate ratio 0.70, 95% CI: 0.58-0.85, P<0.001). Severe exacerbations requiring hospitalization were reduced by 34% (P=0.01). FEV1 improved by 83 mL at week 12 (P<0.001) and was sustained through week 52 (77 mL, P<0.001). SGRQ total score improved by -3.4 points (P=0.001). Benefits were more pronounced in patients with eosinophils ≥350 cells/μL (40% exacerbation reduction). Injection-site reactions (8.4%) and arthralgia (4.2%) were more common with dupilumab. Conclusions: Dupilumab significantly reduces exacerbations and improves lung function in COPD patients with type 2 inflammation, representing the first biologic therapy to demonstrate efficacy in COPD.

title: Machine Learning Prediction of Sepsis Onset in Emergency Department: External Validation Study
abstract: Objective: To externally validate a machine learning model for predicting sepsis onset within 6 hours of emergency department (ED) presentation. Methods: We developed a gradient-boosted decision tree model using 48 features from vital signs, laboratory values, demographics, and nursing assessments from 312,000 ED encounters at 8 development hospitals. External validation was performed on 89,456 encounters at 5 geographically distinct hospitals. Sepsis was defined by Sepsis-3 criteria. Performance was compared against qSOFA and SIRS. Results: The model achieved AUROC 0.89 (95% CI: 0.87-0.91) on external validation, compared with 0.71 for qSOFA and 0.65 for SIRS. At a sensitivity of 85%, specificity was 82.3%, positive predictive value was 31.4%, and negative predictive value was 98.2%. The model identified sepsis a median of 3.2 hours before clinical recognition. Top predictive features were lactate trajectory, heart rate variability, respiratory rate, temperature trend, and bandemia. Alert fatigue analysis showed 2.4 true alerts per false alert at the recommended threshold. Implementation at two pilot sites reduced time to antibiotics by 48 minutes (P<0.001) and 28-day mortality by 2.1 percentage points (P=0.04). Conclusions: The ML sepsis prediction model shows robust external validity and reduces treatment delays when implemented in ED workflows.

title: Tirzepatide Versus Insulin Glargine in Type 2 Diabetes with High Cardiovascular Risk: SURPASS-4 Final Results
abstract: Aims: To report final results of SURPASS-4 comparing tirzepatide, a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist, with insulin glargine in type 2 diabetes patients with high cardiovascular risk. Methods: Adults with T2D, HbA1c 7.5-10.5%, and established cardiovascular disease or high CV risk were randomized to tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg weekly, or insulin glargine titrated to fasting glucose <100 mg/dL. The primary endpoint was HbA1c change at 52 weeks. Safety was assessed through 104 weeks. Results: At 52 weeks, HbA1c reductions were -2.24% (5 mg), -2.43% (10 mg), -2.58% (15 mg) versus -1.44% (glargine) (all P<0.001). At 104 weeks, benefits were sustained. Body weight changed by -6.4 kg, -9.6 kg, -12.9 kg versus +2.3 kg. HbA1c <7.0% was achieved by 87%, 90%, 92% versus 51%. The 4-component MACE occurred in 5.3% (pooled tirzepatide) versus 6.6% (glargine) (HR 0.74, 95% CI: 0.51-1.08). Hypoglycemia (<54 mg/dL) rates were 0.4-0.9% versus 7.2%. Gastrointestinal events decreased over time. Conclusions: Tirzepatide provides superior and durable glycemic control with substantial weight loss compared with insulin glargine in T2D patients with high cardiovascular risk, with a favorable safety profile.
